nodes.label: AI as tool for lesson planning
nodes.label_negative: AI as unreliable or insufficient for nuanced feedback
nodes.grounding_span: I use AI extensively. As a tutor and provider of educational consultancy, I use AI for lesson planning.

nodes.label: AI reliability and factuality
nodes.grounding_span: I also use AI for quick fact checking, especially Perplexity.

nodes.label: AI as efficient quiz/test creator
nodes.label_negative: AI feedback on written assignments (inferior to human)
nodes.grounding_span: I definitely prefer to give feedback on any written assignments myself.

nodes.label: AI as tool for lesson planning
nodes.grounding_span: I'm not sure that AI in its current stage of development will ever entirely replace teachers and tutors, although it can definitely supplement them.

nodes.label: AI as threat to student learning assessment
nodes.label_negative: AI as opportunity to redesign assessments
nodes.grounding_span: we urgently need to think about how we redesign assessments that require students to do research

nodes.label: AI as enhancer of teaching creativity
nodes.label_negative: AI as source of inaccuracy or poor pedagogy
nodes.grounding_span: I was really happy when I got the students to create and play in-text adventure games that explored different historical scenarios.

nodes.label: Preservation of personal intellectual activities (research, writing)
nodes.label_negative: Delegation of all intellectual work to AI
nodes.grounding_span: I will always keep some aspects of research and writing to myself.

nodes.label: AI and child safeguarding concerns
nodes.grounding_span: safeguarding, particularly with younger children. There is no doubt that kids are using AI in multiple interesting ways, but often without any real oversight

nodes.label: Student engagement and learning
nodes.grounding_span: AI has given me a fresh approach to teaching.

nodes.label: Professional autonomy
nodes.grounding_span: I enjoy the intellectual challenge of research and putting together an argument.

nodes.label: Productivity and efficiency
nodes.grounding_span: AI allows me to be very productive and so I will continue to use it to save time

nodes.label: Satisfaction with AI for lesson creation
nodes.grounding_span: I was really happy when I got the students to create and play in-text adventure games

nodes.label: Ambivalent stance toward AI in education
nodes.grounding_span: The disconnect between where AI is and where educational organizations and processes are really concerns me.

nodes.label: Confidence in own writing superiority over AI
nodes.grounding_span: I feel that I can do it faster and better, even if I put the marking criteria into AI.

nodes.label: Openness to AI as supplement but not replacement
nodes.grounding_span: I'm not sure that AI in its current stage of development will ever entirely replace teachers and tutors, although it can definitely supplement them.

nodes.label: Concern about AI and child safeguarding
nodes.grounding_span: safeguarding, particularly with younger children. There is no doubt that kids are using AI in multiple interesting ways, but often without any real oversight

nodes.label: Iterative prompt refinement
nodes.grounding_span: I also work with multiple models to build the perfect prompt, especially if it's an area I don't know much about.

nodes.label: Preliminary non-AI research to inform prompting
nodes.grounding_span: I normally do preliminary non-AI research to provide context for my prompting.
